lessons.title: Flash Characteristics & the Bootloader Jump

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Flash layout showing an immutable bootloader at the low address, an active Slot A application with a header containing magic, size, CRC and signature, an inactive Slot B staging region for new images, and a swap status trailer.

items.front: What does 'erase-before-write' mean for flash, and at what granularity does erase happen?
items.back: Programming can only clear bits (1->0); to set bits back to 1 you must erase, which operates on a whole page/sector (often 2-128 KB), not a single byte. So you cannot rewrite a word in place--you erase its entire page first.
items.hint: Bits only go one way until you erase a whole sector.

items.front: What is flash endurance, and how do you protect frequently-updated data like config/calibration?
items.back: Endurance is the limited number of erase cycles per sector (often ~10k-100k). Protect hot data with wear leveling or EEPROM emulation--spreading writes across many slots/pages rather than repeatedly erasing one page.
items.hint: Spread the writes; don't hammer one page.

items.front: Why does read-while-write limitation matter for a flash-resident updater?
items.back: On many parts the CPU cannot fetch instructions from the same flash bank being erased/programmed. So the update routine must run from RAM (or from a different bank), or the core stalls/faults trying to execute from flash mid-program.
items.hint: You can't execute from the bank you're erasing.

items.front: List the boot flow layers from reset to the application.
items.back: On-chip ROM bootloader (mask ROM) runs at reset and may offer a vendor download mode -> your bootloader in flash, which validates the image -> the application. Each stage hands control to the next.
items.hint: ROM -> your bootloader -> app.

items.front: State the safe four-step sequence a bootloader uses to jump to an application at a higher flash offset.
items.back: 1) Set VTOR to the app's vector table (relocate vectors); 2) load the app's initial SP from vector-table word 0 into MSP; 3) read the reset-handler address from word 1; 4) branch to it (set PC). Disable IRQs and add DSB/ISB barriers around the switch.
items.hint: VTOR, MSP, reset addr, jump.

items.prompt: A bootloader jumps to an app by calling the app's reset handler but never sets VTOR. The app runs until its first interrupt, then crashes. Why?
choices: The app's code was not erased correctly
Interrupts still dispatch through the bootloader's vector table, so the wrong (or stale) handler addresses are used
The stack pointer is automatically wrong without VTOR
VTOR controls the CPU clock needed for interrupts
items.explanation: VTOR tells the core where the active vector table lives. Without relocating it, an interrupt fetches its handler from the bootloader's table--pointing at bootloader code or garbage relative to the running app--so the first IRQ faults. The SP load is a separate step.

items.prompt: You must reprogram a 4 KB region but only one 64 KB sector covers it. What is the correct procedure?
choices: Write the new 4 KB directly over the old bytes
Read-modify the bytes in place one word at a time
Read out the whole sector, erase the sector, then program back the merged 64 KB with the 4 KB changed
Erase only the 4 KB sub-region, then write it
items.explanation: Erase granularity is the whole 64 KB sector, and you cannot set bits without erasing. The safe pattern is read sector to RAM, modify, erase sector, reprogram. You cannot erase a 4 KB sub-region or write over programmed bytes directly.

items.prompt: An app is linked to base 0x08008000. The bootloader reads which words to find the app's initial SP and entry point?
choices: 0x08008000 holds the SP; 0x08008004 holds the reset handler address
0x08008000 holds main()'s address; 0x08008004 holds the SP
Both are stored in RAM at 0x20000000
The SP and PC are read from the bootloader's own vector table
items.explanation: On Cortex-M the vector table begins with the initial stack pointer at offset 0 and the reset-handler (entry) address at offset 4. So at the app base, word 0 is the SP to load into MSP and word 4 is the entry to branch to.

items.prompt: Why do many bootloaders copy the flash erase/program routine into RAM before running an update?
choices: RAM is faster so the update finishes sooner
Some parts cannot execute code from a flash bank while that bank is being erased/programmed (no read-while-write)
Flash routines are encrypted and must be decrypted into RAM
It saves flash endurance on the routine itself
items.explanation: The read-while-write restriction means the CPU may stall or fault fetching instructions from a bank under erase/program. Running the routine from RAM (or a separate bank) avoids executing out of the bank being modified.

lessons.title: Firmware Update (FOTA/DFU): Validate, Swap, Roll Back

items.front: Study this diagram.
items.back: Flowchart of an OTA update: receive image into Slot B, validate CRC and signature, on failure reject and keep running A, on success mark pending and reset, the bootloader swaps and boots the new image which must self-test and confirm, and if no confirm a watchdog or boot counter triggers a revert to A.

items.front: What two properties make a firmware update safe against power loss, and how does the A/B scheme provide them?
items.back: Atomicity and recoverability. The new image is written to the INACTIVE bank while the active one keeps running and is never overwritten in place; a persistent flag plus bootloader swap makes the changeover all-or-nothing, so a mid-update power loss leaves the old working image bootable.
items.hint: Never overwrite the running image in place.

items.front: What is the difference between a CRC check and a signature check on a received image?
items.back: A CRC verifies INTEGRITY (the bits arrived uncorrupted) but anyone can compute it. A signature verifies AUTHENTICITY/integrity against a baked-in public key, proving the image came from a trusted signer--the basis of secure boot. You want both.
items.hint: One catches corruption, the other catches forgery.

items.front: Name the four phases of a robust A/B firmware update.
items.back: 1) Receive the image into the inactive bank over UART/USB-DFU/OTA; 2) Validate with CRC + signature (reject+erase on mismatch); 3) Swap atomically via a persistent pending flag + reset; 4) Confirm after self-test, or roll back (revert) if it fails to confirm.
items.hint: Receive, validate, swap, confirm/rollback.

items.front: How does the confirm/rollback mechanism prevent a bad update from permanently bricking the device?
items.back: After swap, the new image is only 'pending'; it must run a self-test and explicitly confirm. If it crashes or a boot-counter/watchdog expires before confirming, the bootloader reverts to the previous (known-good) slot on the next reset.
items.hint: Pending until proven healthy; watchdog reverts.

items.front: What do MCUboot and ESP-IDF OTA provide so you don't write update logic from scratch?
items.back: MCUboot (used by Zephyr) is an open bootloader with signed images, swap/overwrite strategies, and confirm/revert. ESP-IDF OTA gives esp_ota_* APIs over ota_0/ota_1 partitions with rollback. Both implement validate-swap-confirm with secure boot support.
items.hint: One is the Zephyr standard; one is Espressif's.

items.front: Why include a monotonic version and anti-rollback counter in firmware images?
items.back: To prevent downgrade attacks (re-flashing an older, vulnerable but validly-signed image) and to avoid accidental update loops. The bootloader refuses images whose version is below the stored anti-rollback counter.
items.hint: Stops attackers from re-installing old signed firmware.

items.prompt: Power is lost exactly while the device is writing the new image into the inactive bank. After reboot, what should happen in a correct A/B design?
choices: The device is bricked because the image is half-written
The bootloader runs the half-written inactive image and crashes
The pending flag is not set/confirmed, so the bootloader boots the untouched active image as before
The bootloader erases both banks and waits for a new image
items.explanation: The active image was never touched, and the swap only happens after a fully-written image is validated and the pending flag is committed. An interrupted download leaves the active slot bootable; the device simply restarts the old firmware, which can retry the update.

items.prompt: A new image passes its CRC but its signature does not verify against the device's public key. The correct action is to:
choices: Boot it anyway since the CRC proves it is intact
Reject and erase it; keep running the current image
Store it and ask the user to confirm
Boot it but disable interrupts as a precaution
items.explanation: A valid CRC only proves the bits are uncorrupted, not that they came from a trusted source. A failed signature means the image is unauthenticated (possibly malicious or wrong-key); secure boot must reject it, erase the staging slot, and continue running the trusted image.

items.prompt: After an atomic swap, the new firmware boots but hangs in early init and never calls confirm. With a boot counter + watchdog, what is the safe outcome?
choices: The device stays hung forever in the new image
The bootloader, seeing no confirmation, reverts to the previous slot on the next reset
The bootloader re-downloads the image automatically
Both slots are erased to recover space
items.explanation: The new image is only pending until it confirms. A watchdog reset (or exceeding the boot-attempt counter) without a confirmation tells the bootloader the update is unhealthy, so it reverts to the known-good previous slot--the anti-brick rollback.

items.prompt: Why is writing the incoming image into the INACTIVE bank (rather than over the running app) essential?
choices: The inactive bank is faster to program
It lets the device keep running the current app during download and guarantees a known-good image remains if anything fails
Signatures only validate against the inactive bank
The active bank cannot be erased at all
items.explanation: Staging in the inactive bank means the running app is untouched throughout receive and validation, so a failure or power loss at any point leaves a complete, bootable image. Overwriting the active app in place would leave the device with a partial, unbootable image on interruption.

subtopics.title: DSP on Microcontrollers: the Bridge to Radar

lessons.title: Why DSP Runs on an MCU Now
lessons.content:
There was a time when any real signal processing needed a dedicated DSP chip. That changed when ARM put a single-precision **FPU** and a set of **DSP/SIMD instructions** into the Cortex-M4 (the **M4F**) and a faster pipeline plus optional double-precision FPU into the **Cortex-M7**. These cores can run a multiply-accumulate (MAC) — the heart of every filter and transform — in roughly one cycle, and the SIMD instructions pack two 16-bit operations into one 32-bit datapath.

You do not write those instructions by hand. ARM ships **CMSIS-DSP**, a free, optimized C library of the standard kernels: FIR/IIR filters, FFTs, windowing, vector math, magnitude, correlation, and statistics. It auto-selects SIMD/FPU paths for your core, so the same call runs well on an M0+ (software) or an M4F (hardware FPU + DSP extensions).

The MAC that dominates a length-$M$ FIR filter is

$$ y[n] = \\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k]\\, x[n-k]. $$

A float FIR on an M4F is just a call:

```c
arm_fir_instance_f32 S;
arm_fir_init_f32(&S, M, coeffs, state, BLOCK);
arm_fir_f32(&S, in, out, BLOCK);  /* one MAC per tap, FPU-accelerated */
```

For you this is a homecoming: a radar receiver is timed sampling followed by filtering and an FFT. The MCU is now fast enough to *be* that receiver for low-to-moderate bandwidths. The rest of this subtopic is about doing it without dropping a sample and knowing exactly when the MCU runs out of room.

items.front: What two hardware additions to the Cortex-M4F/M7 made real DSP on a general MCU practical?
items.back: A single-precision (M4F) or double-precision (M7) floating-point unit (FPU), plus DSP/SIMD instructions including single-cycle multiply-accumulate (MAC). These accelerate the MACs at the core of every filter and transform.
items.hint: One does floats fast, one packs parallel integer ops.

items.front: What is CMSIS-DSP and why use it instead of hand-writing kernels?
items.back: ARM's free, optimized C library of standard DSP kernels (FIR/IIR, FFT, windowing, magnitude, correlation, statistics). It auto-selects FPU/SIMD code paths per core, so you get vendor-tuned performance from a portable API instead of writing assembly.
items.hint: Standard library shipped by the core vendor.

items.front: What single operation dominates the cost of an FIR filter, and why does the FPU/SIMD matter for it?
items.back: The multiply-accumulate (MAC): one multiply plus one add per filter tap. With a single-cycle MAC and SIMD packing, an M-tap filter approaches M cycles per output sample instead of many.
items.hint: y[n] = sum of h[k] * x[n-k].

items.front: Why is the MCU DSP pipeline framed as 'the bridge to radar'?
items.back: A radar receiver is fundamentally timed acquisition followed by filtering and FFT/detection. That is exactly the timer->ADC->DMA->window->FFT->magnitude pipeline you build on an MCU, so the same code and the same budgeting reasoning carry straight over.
items.hint: Acquire, filter, transform, detect.

items.front: Does the same CMSIS-DSP call run on a Cortex-M0+ that has no FPU?
items.back: Yes -- the API is the same, but the library compiles to a software (or fixed-point) implementation on cores without an FPU/DSP extension, which is far slower. The portability is in the API, not the performance.
items.hint: Same function name, different code path selected at build.

items.prompt: On a Cortex-M4F, what does the 'F' specifically denote?
choices: A faster flash memory controller
An integrated single-precision floating-point unit (FPU)
A FreeRTOS-certified core variant
A built-in FFT hardware accelerator
items.explanation: The 'F' is the FPU: the M4F adds a single-precision hardware FPU to the Cortex-M4. The M4 also carries DSP/SIMD instructions, but the suffix specifically marks the floating-point unit. There is no dedicated FFT accelerator on a stock M4F.

items.prompt: Which of these is NOT a kernel that CMSIS-DSP provides out of the box?
choices: FIR and IIR (biquad) filtering
Real FFT via arm_rfft_fast
Windowing and complex magnitude/dB
A full TCP/IP networking stack
items.explanation: CMSIS-DSP is a math/signal library: filters, transforms, windowing, magnitude, correlation, statistics. Networking is the domain of stacks like lwIP or a vendor TCP/IP library, not a DSP kernel library.

items.prompt: Why does a single-cycle MAC matter so much for DSP throughput on an MCU?
choices: It eliminates the need for any RAM buffers
Filters and transforms are dominated by multiply-then-accumulate operations, so a 1-cycle MAC roughly sets the per-sample cost
It allows the ADC to sample faster
It removes the need to enable peripheral clocks
items.explanation: FIR/IIR filters and the butterflies of an FFT are sums of products. When each multiply-accumulate retires in one cycle, the core's clock rate translates almost directly into MACs/second, which is the currency of DSP throughput.

items.prompt: A working software developer is told 'this MCU pipeline is just a radar receiver.' What is the most accurate reading of that claim?
choices: It means the MCU can transmit radar pulses at microwave frequencies
It means the acquire-then-filter-then-FFT-then-detect structure is identical, even if the bandwidth and front-end differ
It means radar systems no longer use FPGAs or dedicated DSPs
It means CMSIS-DSP includes radar target-tracking algorithms
items.explanation: The structural pipeline is the same: evenly timed sampling, optional filtering, an FFT, then magnitude/detection. The MCU does not generate RF, and high-bandwidth radars still use FPGAs/DSPs; what transfers is the signal-chain reasoning.

lessons.title: Fixed-Point vs Float, Saturation, and the Core Kernels
lessons.content:
Before the FPU was standard, MCU DSP meant **fixed-point**. CMSIS-DSP still supports it because it is faster on FPU-less cores and cheaper in power. The two common formats are **Q15** (a 16-bit signed value scaled so the range is $[-1, 1)$ with $2^{-15}$ resolution) and **Q31** (the 32-bit version). A real number $x$ is stored as the integer $\\mathrm{round}(x \\cdot 2^{15})$ for Q15.

The trap is overflow. Multiplying two Q15 values gives a Q30 product needing 32 bits; adding many of them (as a filter does) can exceed the format. Plain two's-complement arithmetic **wraps**: $+0.99$ can suddenly become $-1.0$, a catastrophic spike. The DSP instructions instead **saturate** — clamp to the max/min — which is graceful degradation, exactly like an overdriven analog stage clipping rather than folding. CMSIS-DSP's `_q15`/`_q31` kernels use saturating MACs.

Float (`_f32`) sidesteps scaling entirely: huge dynamic range, no manual Q-format bookkeeping, at the cost of more cycles and energy than fixed-point on the same core. On an M4F float is usually the right default; reach for Q15/Q31 only when the budget is tight or the core lacks an FPU.

The core kernels you will compose:

```c
arm_rfft_fast_instance_f32 fft;
arm_rfft_fast_init_f32(&fft, 1024);          /* real 1024-pt FFT */
arm_mult_f32(samples, hann, windowed, 1024); /* apply window      */
arm_rfft_fast_f32(&fft, windowed, freq, 0);  /* 0 = forward       */
arm_cmplx_mag_f32(freq, mag, 512);           /* |bin| magnitude   */
```

Windowing (Hann, Hamming, Blackman) reduces spectral leakage before the FFT; magnitude and a $20\\log_{10}$ step give a dB spectrum; correlation (`arm_correlate_f32`) is matched-filtering — the same operation a pulse-compression radar performs on its return.

items.front: What range and resolution does Q15 represent, and how is a real value stored?
items.back: Q15 is a 16-bit signed fixed-point value covering [-1, 1) with resolution 2^-15. A real number x is stored as the integer round(x * 2^15). Q31 is the same idea in 32 bits with resolution 2^-31.
items.hint: 16 bits, normalized to roughly +/-1.

items.front: What is the difference between wrapping and saturating arithmetic on overflow, and why does DSP prefer saturation?
items.back: Wrapping rolls a near-max positive value over to a large negative one (a destructive spike). Saturation clamps to the format's max/min. DSP prefers saturation because clipping degrades gracefully -- like an overdriven analog stage -- instead of injecting a wild discontinuity.
items.hint: Clip vs roll-over.

items.front: When should you pick float (_f32) over fixed-point (Q15/Q31) on an MCU, and vice versa?
items.back: Use float on an FPU-equipped core (M4F/M7) as the default: huge dynamic range, no Q-format bookkeeping. Use Q15/Q31 when there is no FPU, when power/cycles are tight, or to fit narrow memory -- accepting manual scaling and overflow management.
items.hint: Float = convenience + range; fixed = speed/power on weak cores.

items.front: Why apply a window (Hann/Hamming/Blackman) before an FFT?
items.back: A finite, non-integer-period capture has discontinuities at its edges that smear energy across bins (spectral leakage). A tapered window forces the block to roll off smoothly at both ends, concentrating energy near the true frequency at the cost of slightly wider main lobes.
items.hint: Reduces leakage from abrupt block edges.

items.front: What radar operation is CMSIS-DSP correlation (arm_correlate) equivalent to?
items.back: Matched filtering / pulse compression: correlating the received signal against a known transmitted template maximizes SNR and locates the echo. The same correlation kernel that finds a code in comms finds a chirp return in radar.
items.hint: Match the return against the transmitted pulse.

items.prompt: You multiply two Q15 numbers near +0.9. Without saturation, what is the danger when accumulating many such products in a filter?
choices: The result silently wraps to a large negative value, producing a spurious spike
The FPU automatically rescales to float
The values are rounded to zero
Nothing -- Q15 cannot overflow
items.explanation: Two's-complement accumulation wraps on overflow: a value past +full-scale reappears as a large negative number, injecting a discontinuity. Saturating MACs (the _q15 kernels) clamp instead, which is why they are used for filters.

items.prompt: On a Cortex-M4F running a 1024-point real FFT, which choice is usually the sensible default and why?
choices: Q15, because fixed-point is always faster than float
f32, because the hardware FPU makes float fast and removes Q-format scaling bugs
Q31, because 32-bit integers are required for any FFT
Double precision, because the M4F FPU is double-precision
items.explanation: The M4F has a single-precision FPU, so f32 is fast and avoids the manual scaling/overflow management of fixed-point. (Q15/Q31 win only when cycles/power/memory are tight or there is no FPU; the M4F FPU is single-, not double-precision.)

items.prompt: What is the purpose of arm_cmplx_mag_f32 in the spectrum pipeline?
choices: It applies the window function to the time-domain samples
It computes the magnitude sqrt(re^2 + im^2) of each complex FFT bin to give the spectrum amplitude
It triggers the next DMA transfer
It converts Q15 samples to float
items.explanation: The rFFT produces complex bins; arm_cmplx_mag_f32 collapses each to its magnitude sqrt(re^2+im^2), the per-bin amplitude. A following 20*log10 step turns that into a dB spectrum.

items.prompt: arm_rfft_fast_f32 is preferred over a generic complex FFT for ADC data because:
choices: ADC samples are real-valued, so a real FFT exploits Hermitian symmetry to roughly halve the work and memory
It is the only FFT that supports windowing
Complex FFTs cannot run on a Cortex-M
It outputs results in decibels directly
items.explanation: Real input has a Hermitian-symmetric spectrum, so a real FFT computes only the unique half of the bins, using about half the operations and storage of a full complex FFT on the same N. It does not window or convert to dB for you.

lessons.title: The Real-Time Pipeline and the Compute Budget
lessons.content:
Now assemble the whole chain and prove it runs in real time. A **timer** triggers the **ADC** at a precise rate $f_s$; the **DMA** streams conversions into a **double (ping-pong) buffer** of $N$ samples per half. When a half fills, a half-transfer/transfer-complete interrupt hands that buffer to the CPU, which applies a window, runs an $N$-point `arm_rfft_fast_f32`, and computes magnitude — *while DMA fills the other half*. No sample is dropped because acquisition and processing run on disjoint buffers in parallel.

The budget is a deadline race. A block of $N$ samples arrives every

$$ T_{\\text{block}} = \\frac{N}{f_s}. $$

Processing that block costs some cycles $C$; you must satisfy

$$ C < T_{\\text{block}} \\cdot f_{\\text{cpu}} \\quad\\Longleftrightarrow\\quad \\frac{C}{N} < \\frac{f_{\\text{cpu}}}{f_s}. $$

An $N$-point FFT costs on the order of $N\\log_2 N$ operations, so per-sample cost grows like $\\log_2 N$. The tension: a **larger $N$** gives finer frequency resolution (bin spacing $f_s/N$) and better processing gain, but costs more cycles per block and adds latency $T_{\\text{block}}$; a **higher $f_s$** captures more bandwidth (Nyquist $f_s/2$) but shrinks the deadline. You cannot independently maximize bandwidth, resolution, and CPU headroom — that triangle is the whole game, and it is *exactly* the radar designer's range-resolution / unambiguous-range / dwell-time tradeoff.

```c
void DMA1_Stream0_IRQHandler(void) {
  if (half_transfer) { process_block(&buf[0]); }   /* CPU on half A */
  else               { process_block(&buf[N]); }   /* CPU on half B */
  /* must finish before this half refills: deadline = N / fs */
}
```

When even the M7 cannot keep up — wider bandwidth, larger FFTs, multiple channels — you move processing to a dedicated **DSP**, an **FPGA** (streaming, deterministic, massively parallel), or an **application processor** running Linux (handoff to 05). Knowing *where that line is* for your $f_s$, $N$, and $f_{\\text{cpu}}$ is the engineering judgment this whole module builds toward.

items.front: In the real-time spectrum pipeline, why does a DMA double (ping-pong) buffer prevent dropped samples?
items.back: The CPU processes the half that just filled while the DMA continues writing the other half. Acquisition and processing run in parallel on disjoint buffers, so no incoming sample has to wait for the CPU and none is overwritten before it is read.
items.hint: Process one half while the other fills.

items.front: Write the per-block deadline for processing N samples acquired at rate fs, and state the constraint that must hold.
items.back: T_block = N / fs. Processing cost C (in cycles) must satisfy C < T_block * f_cpu, equivalently C/N < f_cpu/fs. If processing a block takes longer than a block takes to fill, you fall behind and drop data.
items.hint: Time to fill a block vs cycles to process it.

items.front: State the three-way tradeoff between FFT size N, sample rate fs, and CPU budget.
items.back: Larger N: finer bins (fs/N) and more processing gain, but more cycles/block and more latency. Higher fs: more bandwidth (Nyquist fs/2) but a shorter deadline N/fs. Finite f_cpu caps cycles/block. You cannot maximize bandwidth, resolution, and headroom at once.
items.hint: Resolution vs bandwidth vs cycles -- a triangle.

items.front: What is the frequency resolution (bin spacing) of an N-point FFT taken at sample rate fs?
items.back: fs / N hertz per bin. Doubling N halves the bin spacing (finer resolution) but doubles the block time N/fs (more latency) and increases cycles per block.
items.hint: Span fs over N bins.

items.front: When do you move processing off the MCU to a DSP, FPGA, or application processor?
items.back: When the per-block deadline can no longer be met -- wider bandwidth (higher fs), larger FFTs, or many channels push C/N above f_cpu/fs. FPGAs give deterministic streaming parallelism; app processors (Linux) give throughput and software ecosystem; both are the handoff beyond the MCU.
items.hint: When C/N exceeds f_cpu/fs even on an M7.

items.prompt: You sample at fs = 200 kHz with an FFT size N = 1024. What is the approximate per-block deadline the processing must beat?
choices: About 5.12 ms
About 5.12 us
About 51.2 ms
About 200 ms
items.explanation: T_block = N / fs = 1024 / 200000 s = 5.12 ms. The window + FFT + magnitude for that block must complete within 5.12 ms, or the next half-buffer overwrites unprocessed data.

items.prompt: Doubling N from 1024 to 2048 at a fixed fs has which combined effect?
choices: Bin spacing halves (finer resolution), but block latency doubles and cycles/block more than double
Bin spacing doubles and latency halves
Nothing changes except memory usage
Nyquist frequency doubles
items.explanation: Bin spacing fs/N halves, improving resolution. Block time N/fs doubles, raising latency. FFT cost ~N*log2(N) more than doubles. Nyquist (fs/2) depends only on fs, so it is unchanged.

items.prompt: Why does this exact MCU pipeline justify the label 'a radar receiver'?
choices: Because it generates the transmitted RF pulse

Because timed acquisition -> windowing -> FFT/correlation -> magnitude/detection is the core structure of a radar signal chain, and the N/fs/CPU tradeoff mirrors range-resolution/ambiguity/dwell tradeoffs
Because CMSIS-DSP includes a CFAR detector
Because DMA is a radar-specific peripheral
items.explanation: The receiver structure and the budgeting math are the same: even sampling, optional matched filtering/windowing, an FFT, then magnitude/detection, governed by a resolution-vs-bandwidth-vs-compute tradeoff. The MCU does not produce RF, and DMA/CMSIS-DSP are general-purpose.

items.prompt: The processing of a block consistently takes 6 ms but a block fills every 5 ms. What is the correct response?
choices: Increase fs so blocks fill faster
Reduce the workload per block (smaller N, lighter window, or fewer ops) or lower fs, or move processing to a DSP/FPGA/app processor
Disable the DMA double buffer
Add more flash memory
items.explanation: Processing time (6 ms) exceeds the deadline (5 ms), so you are losing the race. Fixes: shrink N, simplify the kernels, or lower fs to relax the deadline -- and if none suffice, offload to a DSP/FPGA/application processor. Raising fs makes the deadline worse, not better.
